turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: -- to wind up and go into Court all the time. These litigants do not have big pockets to pay expenses and so you have the proposition which would affect Mr. Nizer's case more than mine but if the local exhibitor owed in contest within the pictures classified and would there have to bear the --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Do you think --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: -- marketing.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Do you think the non-contesting part of it should come after when a complaint is filed in the Court?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well, I think as soon as --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Like every -- every picture classified is none suitable -- it's not suitable for young children should have a complaint filed on it in a court?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: If the -- yes sir.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: And a -- and a default judgment entered?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: No sir. The default judgment certainly should be taken but that leaves again free speech unprotected because a man takes one of these pictures and has a limited license a small fee --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: -- then would you prefer some mechanism by -- Court and have a judgment entered that you could keep it out of Court? Wouldn't you like some proceeding like that?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: That is one that Mr. Nizer --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: What seems to be what you're complaining about is some --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: No sir, what I'm complaining about if I may say is this that they talked about Freedman and they changed their position on Freedman. Freedman's overall burden mentioned that prior Maryland case, that's the real thrust of this argument. There, it mentioned Maryland versus United Artist and Board of Censors in 1956 and it admit -- it pointed out that the risk of delay was built into the only reported case in Maryland because it took six months from trial to final decision. Now, six months was what mistaken there, the record of Viva Maria is eight-and-a-half months.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: You mean from the time of the adminis -- at the beginning of the administrative proceeding?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Suit was filed in a Maryland Censor case on February 28th and July 13th.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, now I'm talking about Texas --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: My case was eight-and-a-half months.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, that appears through the -- that appears through the Supreme Court of Texas.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: That is correct, Justice. The final judicial determination, if the Court please, in Texas.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, and what's your Freedman means then?

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: What was it -- the interval from the time this picture was filed with this Board until the February 18th date when the trial court entered its judgment was how many days.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: February 18th, the picture was filed with the Board on the 9th of February, I believe.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: So that's nine days then from the time of filing with the Board until the trial court determination?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Now, forgetting the other aspects on which you challenged --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- the Court, as a time interval, do you think that would come within Freedman in nine days?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: If the Court please, I would say, it would be close enough, I wouldn't argue.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Yes. So, on the time then your real complain I gather is that it then took upwards to better than eight months --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- before the appellate process was completed.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: That is true.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Now, do you find anything in Freedman that purports to say the states that they must so order their appellate processes as to give appellate review in these cases on a priority basis?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: I would think so, if the Court please.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Was there any thing in Freedman that says that?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: I would think so. Toward the end of the opinion, this Court did not only used words idly --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, I used it

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: -- and I understand that. But from final, the word “final” that came in here was expelled out. Also, the opinion states to avoid the danger of an interim and possibly erroneous denial of a license. Now, an interim is possibly erroneous denial of a license is exactly what may have happened to us here in Judge Taft's decision at the trial level. But this term final judicial determination was understood by Maryland to advance and require an amendment of its statute which is done, that's reported in Trans-Lux that is cited in my brief. Now, --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, what if --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: If I may --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: -- would you say that -- could the Texas just say there will be no appeals at all under these cases from the trial court decision?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well, they could but we have the unique situation, Your Honor. The legislature was in session in 1967, when Freedman came out, they did nothing. But in 1967, they were in session again and on page 17 of my brief we have been managing general insurance agent site. And that thing gives the priority to an insurance salesman's license. The likes of which the First Amendment should get, it goes to the first docket at the trial court, the Court of Civil Appeals and the Supreme Court of Texas --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, I'm just wondering --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: -- I mean, no worries (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: If there is another -- if there isn't another problem involved here, on what can depend any order of this Court to the states that they must so order their appellate procedures in their highest courts as to determine appellate review in these cases before they determine anything else, capital cases or other cases that --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- how do we do that?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: -- I think it can leave Freedman as it was. The Court there said it was not spending out the exact time but the spirit of Freedman was a prompt final judicial determination.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: And why does the City Council of Dallas doesn't have any power whatsoever to tell the Supreme Court of Texas --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- as that you lack in any case, does it?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: The exactly true if the Court please. But if the State of Texas is what said about in Freedman and then Maryland how or whether Maryland is to incorporate the required procedural safeguards in the statutory scheme is of course for the State of Maryland to decide. That's exactly true for Texas if they won't to avail themselves this. The legislature could give these cases the priority equivalent to what it gave to the insurance agent. That's all we're asking for from this -- when state policy gives the priority an insurance agent's license and it literally gives to the head of everything throughout the state.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Of course the Freedman case involved, did it not, the denial of a license to exhibit the movie?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: This case is not at all, is it? (Voice Overlap) exhibit this movie and play it everyday for six months in Dallas ground out your projectors 24 hours a day as far as I know if you just put on it not suitable for children.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well, if you put on it not suitable for children and we define a young person and define what they are in the broad principle as I mentioned earlier if the Court please. In the broad principle, what is the difference between not suitable for 18 and not suitable for 16? If I were to judge by standard or ask anyone else to how would I sensibly measure a difference and consider this book would pass for 18 but this book would not pass for 16.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, there's some arbitrary about every age limit, how much differences there in voting between somebody who's 20 years and 11 months and 29 days and somebody who has a day older and yet that makes a four-year difference and when he can vote in a presidential election.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: That is true if the Court please. I certainly agree with that but we're not called upon as we understand this is so restricted because --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: And this Court has never held that there was anything wrong about that 18-year-old evidence as ordinance, has it?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: No. They may --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: I don't quite see the relevance of your question, your hypothetical question.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well, the relevance --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: In between in 18 and a 16-year-old cut off date. This Court has never held that there's anything wrong with the 18.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: We say that relevance is this, if the Court please, we come to a relevant item because the City of Dallas set out to come under your clear-and-present-danger doctrine for 18 year olds, and obviously people who would be in a juvenile crime group would be at the senior part of this, they fell away from the 18 standard and went to 16. 16 is a little more appealing, but if we sustain this preset which they're holding for here, then we can immediately find 18, 19, or whatever age on up unto 21. In the broad principle until there is some showing, we submit that it is time for -- enough to prove this before and to prove for example if there is such proof that this causes this crime way where there are some reason for restraint, but until that is done a presumption in favor of free speech. That's what we're asking.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, of course the First Amendment, I suppose, philosophically you would agree depends and grows from the concept of free choice. Anybody is free to say or print what he wants and anybody is free to listen or to read what he wants.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: And free choice, I suppose, is that philosophy is on the premise of the capacity to make free choice to make choices. And I suppose you and I would both agree that a child of 18 months doesn't have that capacity, wouldn't he?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Certainly sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: So there must be some period at which the First Amendment is no longer --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- so long this (Voice Overlap) of you argument?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: -- we, I did not come here arguing an 18-year-old child that has the right to go to the picture show by himself very obviously but I wouldn't say --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. Hartt, wouldn't you think that an 18-year-old GI from Saigon that not be corrupted by a movie.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: I would certainly concur in that opinion, Your Honor.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: It could not be the reason it got --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Couldn't that be the reason they got from --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: That is certainly one of the big reasons obviously.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: I mean --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: -- in this question of time, do you take the position that the State of Texas in order to uphold this ordinance must get precedent to your case over a speedy trial in Murdock?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir. In the sense, I would say --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well, for this simple reason.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: I think I read somebody's speedy trial in a certain constitution?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir and I read that --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: This is not in the Constitution

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: I read -- I have read that that is if I understand it correctly that concept is written into a prompt final judicial determination here. Now, I must say Mr.--

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: What is the final judicial determination? Didn't you get a final judgment in the trial court?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: We'll, we've got a judgment --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) the final judgment?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: But that is not the final judgment as we understand --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: What's your final judgment?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: It was an appealable judgment, in that sense, the final, yes.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: And it had to be final.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: It was final in that sense. But at the same time, when they spoke of the appellate judgment in the Maryland case of 1956, if the Court please, we understood that to mean both trial and appellate when they have said six months for trial in appellate --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: But in today, isn't it an eight-month determination by the highest court of the states speedy?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: I would think it is tremendous if the Court please.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: This is final then either then in your term, is it?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well, I think that Freedman given a reason and interpretation means that --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, how can you cut it off the State Supreme Court if you're going to cut the appellate process?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: You don't cut it off there, but you have to review there.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, I know but you aren't through yet.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: You're up here now.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: That is true. But the determination --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, may I ask this Mr. Hartt?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Let's see how this -- what your approach concretely is. Obviously, the City of Dallas could not include in any ordinance, I gather, these municipal ordinances.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir, it does not have --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: And it could not have a provision that any appellate review must be within X number of months.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: It couldn't be done that. So what you're saying, I gather, is that this ordinance we should say must be inoperative, however otherwise, it maybe constitutional. It must be declared completely inoperative unless and until the legislature of the State of Texas --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- says that any judgment under this ordinance must be reviewed and finally determined by the Supreme Court of Texas within some specified period.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Reasonable time.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, anyway isn't that a difference in good sense between the way one would look at Freedman in terms of a total suppression or something, in a way one would look at Freedman in terms of an ordinance. It says that children can't be allowed to view this. Isn't there qualitative difference there? Even if you assume, isn't it conceivable that you could have an arrangement that might offend Freedman if it were total suppression do you see? In one hand, but would not offend Freedman if it were suppression that was confined to excluding children?

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Your Honor, I frankly don't think that you can consistently do it. I personally don't know. Well let's --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Suppose this ordinance --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: I beg your pardon, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Suppose this ordinance that you provided instead of the effect that it does, it's something that deals, no parent in Texas should carry his son who is 20-and-a-half years old to see a picture that a Board in Dallas thinks is bad, what would you say about that law as to the rights of the father? Could he be convicted criminally by taking his boy to that theatre?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Oh, I think he could be under that kind of an ordinance or statute, yes sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Now, do you say again it's a valid ordinance?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You would think that's a valid ordinance?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: No. I say -- excuse me, I understood -- can he be convicted, I thought you asked me, yes he could be convicted unless you're going to follow and say that free speech is going to prevail. The ordinance I think has got to fall on the first --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But another thing comes in mind, it's the right of the parent for the children and the right to the government for the children.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: And also the rights of the children as the Gault case --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Yes, but this ordinance allows even as to this part -- this motion picture, as I understand it there's any provision in this ordinance that one under 16 may see the picture if taken to the picture by parent, guardian, wife, or spouse or something?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: If the Court please --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: 16 year olds who are married can go?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir. That's the normal exception but your --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, I know. But addressed to Mr. Justice Black's question --

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- this ordinance or am I wrong about that?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: The technical reading of this ordinance as pointed out by Mr. Nizer yesterday makes that an affirmative defense only. We have never --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: In a prosecution?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: We have never gotten around to that part --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Or any other proceeding whatever that means?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Yes. Yes, that's where we were, yesterday.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Suppose this 20-and-a-half year old man happen to be married?

turns.name: GROVER HARTT, JR.
turns.text: Well, if he had his wife with him --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do you think his wife would be considerate? And she said all the words through the picture, Your Honor, I think that would be a defense.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Bickley.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice Warren and Associate Justices, may I address myself to the latest question that was just raised. This is about the right of the parent and the right of children. This ordinance does provide, as we read it and as we interpret it and as the city council passed, that a parent, a guardian, a husband, or wife may take a child or a young person below the age of 16 years to a movie that is designated as not acceptable for young persons.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: It provides that now?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It provides that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: It has the right to provide that they may take their children, why does a man have a right to provide that they shall not take their children?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black, I believe Mr. Nizer stated yesterday that in his opinion they did have that right maybe to prohibit any of them from going any used as an example of pool halls which he likened to the right of assembly and he said you could prevent young persons from assembling in a pool hall.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: You said, you could prevent them from attending any movie.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes, he said that.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Because you couldn't pick and choose the movie --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: But you could not --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: -- that he can see?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- pick and choose, this is correct.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: That's what I understood you say.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Alright, yes, and I -- this is what I'd like to address myself to.He said but he likened it to the pool halls but he said that he would not accept the pool hall analogy because of the fact that this was based upon safety of the young person. Now, it is with this argument that I would take disagreement because of the fact that preventing young persons from going in a pool hall is not a safety provision. There is no danger of falling off a pool table or being injured in the pool hall. It's the immorality that this is actually directed to because the YMCA's have pools in many cities and allow individuals to play young persons, to participate but under certain conditions. Now, insofar as that applies here as to whether or not you can pick and choose, we think that actually it would be more unconstitutional not to pick and choose and it would to pick and choose for this simple reason, to prevent young persons under the age of 16 years from attending any movie would be the most blatant sort of the restriction on the freedom of speech.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. Bickley, what's wrong with this picture?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Justice --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Have you seen it by the way?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Have you seen it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I have seen the picture, yes sir on two or three different occasions.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: What's wrong with it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Justice Marshall, let me say this about this picture, it is a marginal picture. I must admit this to this Court and I'll be very frank in this respect.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, what is on the other side of the margin?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: As was pointed out by Reverend Masio (ph) and I agree with him in this respect, it depicts sexual promiscuity in this manner. It shows a woman who goes and under the influence of alcohol meets three men for the first time in an evening and goes and has an evening with them on all evening escapade which is not shown, comes back in the morning with their closed pool half way off and they shout. And she comes back and she says, “This is the life or this is the fun I have been missing”.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: What's wrong with that?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, maybe -- for some maybe it's not wrong, Mr. Chief Justice -- Mr. Justice Marshall but --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: But for example --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I apologize.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: -- is it obscene?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I apologize.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Is it obscene?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No sir. It is not obscene.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, what is it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It is implanting in the minds of the young people the thought to see the play that immorality of this sort -- of this kind --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Is fun?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- is fun and the highest form of life.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I thought you said it was marginal --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- and desirable, and useful.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I thought you said it was marginal.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I say it is marginal, yes sir.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: And is it your -- is it the position of the City of Dallas that you can apply this statute in what you style marginal situations of this ordinance -- on what you style marginal situations?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Justice Fortas, we would prefer not to apply them the marginal situation.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I know, I'm not asking --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- what you prefer doing. I'm asking you whether it's your position that under this ordinance the prohibitions of this ordinance maybe applied in marginal situations. Remember, they were talking about the First Amendment of the Constitution --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I understand that and --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- of the United States.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- and this is the reason I hope to answer very carefully --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- on this. It is hard to determine sometimes and I think in cases of doubt --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Well, it's fairly easy to supply before decision that sounds marginal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I suppose you to say that as far as the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It was an eight to one decision.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Eight to one?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Federal Constitution is concerned, marginal situation is exactly the kind of thing that both parties supposed to deal with.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, marginal situations are the ones that make lawsuits and that's the reason we're before this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) Federal Constitution of the United States is not before this Court or the Board of Censorship and how we felt, but we're talking about the Constitution of the United States --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's exactly right sir. That's exactly right.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: But there are nine -- there are nine censors down there, there in Dallas?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Only nine?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: They are not censors --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: No?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- as we refer to them Your Honor but members of the Classification Board.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: But five -- five to four decision would ban one of these movies?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It would actually do so.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It would not ban it sir. It would not prohibit it. It merely requires a label in the advertising.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: That's your answer to my question? I say that's your answer to my question?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mu anwer to your question, Your Honor, is we do not prohibit anything. We merely say, let's have it's somewhat near the truth in advertising sort of situation, it's somewhat near the situation of pick and choose if you will but give them something from which to pick and choose.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I'm asking you the following --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: So, let us assume that this Board said to the city attorney or whoever its counsel is, we believe that this motion picture is marginal for purposes of application of this ordinance, for purposes of application of this ordinance. In view of our conclusion that this is marginal, is it alright for us to go again and apply the ordinance to this picture? I'm asking that --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: In --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- our view --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- as a matter of law.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: May a city attorney and may I answer you sir in an example which is a true example on the occasions in which I have before this Board actually fought with them and told them what this Court has held and what we think because of marginal situations they could not do and should not do and they have agreed to that and they have many instances, done things they did not want to do. Personally, they disagreed with for this reason and we take this thing very seriously and for that reason we have attempted, it is true, we've got a situation here that may make some bad law because it is the marginal case. But we have attempted to counsel with the Board and to work with them as officers of the Court and attempt to protect these First Amendment freedoms as best as we can but at the same time giving to the state the right to protect its young persons, if we can do that. And we think that maybe this --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: But particularly, in terms of the provisions of this ordinance, in terms of what it's aimed that preventing young people from seeing the way you describe the scene doesn't sound marginal in terms of the ordinance. The ordinance supposedly attempts to keep young people from seeing precisely the kinds of things that you have said has earned this picture. Now, what's marginal? What so marginal about -- about these pictures because there's only one scene in the whole picture?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Is that it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No. No, because there are only a very few scenes in the whole picture. Another one of the scenes that I did not described, Your Honor, was one that occurred inside the -- inside the jail --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: With the prisoners?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: One or two more.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Is this ordinance -- this ordinance, the way it's been applied or does it say on its face that a picture may be classified this way if it's in whole or in part.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It says in whole taken as a whole, using the test that has been set out by this Court, that it must be considered as a whole.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: If the picture -- if this picture were had a -- just a repetitious, just a whole of a lot of these sort of thing that you've been telling us about, you wouldn't think it would be marginal now?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, I would not think it was marginal if it -- if this was the whole theme of the movie as such.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But here you mean it's marginal only in the sense that applying the standard that's taken as a whole, these few incidents may not completely colored as -- as would perhaps whole series of it.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: This is right Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Is that it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And it -- it may -- the standards are hard to apply as the obscenity standard is hard to apply, none of these are easy as counsel admit -- and if they're hard to draw, they're hard to define, and for this reason it does make for a hard --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: But in terms of the quality of this scene, you think it's the kind of thing the ordinance does? But thoroughly is the kind of a thing the ordinance was attempting to reach?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Justice, if you say thoroughly, the kind of thing it was entitled to --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well I mean -- let's say it's -- it's not a marginal?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: If it were not marginal I think this is one of the things that --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: What happens if the scene itself in terms of quality or its content is not marginal?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, the scene by itself is not. The movie as a whole is.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Is the reason that the Board did not make any findings, the reason that the Board relied on as a whole phase?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes, the Board --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: That's correct, am I not? The Board did not give any reasons.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's correct, they did not. They did not.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: But do they ever?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, they do not write down the reasons for which they do it, Mr. Justice Marshall.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: They --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: --how can the distributor or United Artist or this Court know the reason?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: For which they actually did it?

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor before this Court will be confronted with that situation it must be proven before a judicial body.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And the decision will have to be made there.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, I understand only two members of the Board testified.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Or do we know now the reason that the other six voted?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It is not in the record, Your Honor.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, is there any way for us to know?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: How?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I can advise you, but this is the only way.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: But that would be hearsay rule, isn't it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Sir.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: That would be hearsay rule.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes, it would be hearsay. It would be hearsay. And I say for that reason it's not in the record and it does not show. But this is the immaterial to a situation like this because of the fact that judge has to make a decision on a trial de novo.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But he gave no reasons either, did he?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: He gave no reasons at that particular time. No he did not accept (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Isn't this the case where the judge brought in from some outlying county?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: The judge came in from an outlying county.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: And then --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: And he's the one who said, “I've got too much work back at my -- back at home to be fussing around with this, it's enough and let it go”, wasn't that it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, that's not quite it, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: The record certainly reads like that, they tried very hard to get them the status -- to make some findings of fact then he refused to make any.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's correct, (Voice Overlap) he refused to make findings of fact as such and he did not make them. He did view the movie. He did listen to the testimony. He did listen to argument of counsel and he did then make a decision that --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: When he had the standards of the statute before him?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: He had the standards of a statute, he had briefs of counsel, he had all of the consideration that counsel could give him and all of the advice and the argument that either of us could give to him at that time.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, here's what he said, “I decline when I asked. I have so many irons for little fellow. I have taken on more than I can do trying to decide a big case here and I've got others at home and here and then Hill County where I have been helping out. I don't have the time to do it, I decline.” That's what he said.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's exactly what he said Your Honor. But we had been arguing with him for quite some time before he said this, and had been presenting ideas back and forth very freely.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, is this -- is this the one then? “I realize you gentlemen might be right”, I guess he was talking to interstate counsel.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes, he was.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: “There are two or three features in this picture that look to me would be unsuitable to young people, actually they have tried to track that Freedman case looks like in this ordinance and if any ordinance can be passed where it looks like they have taken every ground to pass whether one can be valid, this is valid”. That's what it was.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, I think he tried to follow the law as best he could understand it.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: There is something to what you say.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do you think anybody can understand it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes. Sir, I'm -- I'm not really sure of what the understanding is when we get in these nebulous fields, they're hard, and they are difficult. And so we are all trying to approach that thing which gives the rights of both parties proper precedent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Can you tell me (Voice Overlap) how many movies have come to this Board a year (Inaudible)?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes sir. Between January 14th of 1966 and January 10th of 1968, there were 1925 movies submitted to the Board. Of that number, 565 of them were classified as not suitable by the exhibitor himself. He asked for that classification to be put on. 39 were classified as not suitable after screening by the Board itself and of the movies classified suitable, 1226 of them were classified suitable as submitted by the exhibitor and 59 of them were classified suitable after screening by the Committee and by the Classification Board.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are those figures in your brief?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Those figures are not in the brief sir. Those figures are just of recent vantage. They -- actually we got those on January 10th, 1968. We would like to submit them to the Court for their consideration in the record. Thank you.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do you have any idea in your experience and observation with the Board, how many would've been held bad? And hadn't been for lawyers like you to tell them this Court, there, in the First Amendment protected a lot of large motion pictures, do you think it would have been anything like a smaller number?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr.--

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: If you just left them (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black, I'd hate to speculate with you on that particular statement, I do not know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Board was never on a position to tell this Court in passing the statute. This is the first time spent -- this is the first time that we ever had it.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: This is the first time we've ever had an ordinance similar to this.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) as to what the attitude of this Court would be, wouldn't it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Not as to this. We --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Now, let me --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- discussed very frankly --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Let me correct you (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I didn't place that question, as to what you had told them about --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I understand sir. I understand.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What I see, have you not been warned that as a First Amendment which does protect certain kinds of pictures, can you prophesize how many more they would have barred than those they have already barred?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: May I answer it this way, Mr. Justice Black. I could no more predict than I could predict without benefit of counsel how much smut and other type of things would be introduced into our system because it's a two-way proposition.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I accept your answer but I am sure that you can hold and it would have been a lot more, I heard your statement about what you had told them from time to time in your argument but -- how about the Constitution?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It's possible there would've been more sir, it's possible. Insofar as the speedy remedy is concerned and the final judicial determination is concerned which was being discussed just a minute ago, I'd like to discuss it in a minute. In these words, a careful reading of the ordinance of the City of Dallas will I think convince this Court of the very onerous burden that the city accepted upon itself in order that it might provide every remedy known and every opportunity for a speedy trial and speedy judicial determination. Now, if final judicial determination as used in the Freedman case means by the highest court of the land and without appeal, then this body itself is the one the must give precedents to all these cases because this is the final bargain. And so I think the case certainly did not mean that. We get a final judgment before that trial court. We get a judgment that is final and can be appealed from. And that is a judicial determination. Now, as to whether or not it's unconstitutional to require the movie industry, the exhibitors, as such to file a notice of nonacceptance before we go before that judicial tribunal. I think this Court would not put upon us the burden of doing a vain fame because the movie industry itself does not walk the default judgment before the Court which --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- says they tried --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- I think Mr. Bickley, don't you, that that requirement of filing of notice of nonacceptance is surely inconsistent with some of the things said in the Freedman case.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Said in what? In the Freedman?

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: In Freedman.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: In the Freedman case?

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Yes, isn't it? Or is it inconsistent with the emphasis that Freedman gave to the burden being upon the government to proceed to the initial determination by a judge? Because as I understand it, the way this works if whoever suppose to file the -- and it's definitely -- who may file a notice of nonacceptance besides the exhibitor? Is it only the chap who submits the picture for classification?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, it maybe filed any exhibitor by any city.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Any exhibitor?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: In the city.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: In a city?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But would this then --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- United Artist could not file?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Or United Artist -- the ordinance does not say that United Artist might do so. We would assume that since they have a financial --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: I know but --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- interest in the picture that they could do so, yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: No. Oh! You assume it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Oh, yes. We would accept that as a nonacceptance. But the ordinance doesn't say this, does it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: The ordinance does not actually say that --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Or it doesn't say it can't either, does it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It does not say it cannot because the only mode of speech from -- with United Artist in the City of Dallas is through its exhibitor relationship.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But the ordinance makes the failure to file the notice of nonacceptance that makes them, what do you call this Board, Classification Board?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Classification Board.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Determination, final doesn't it? Never again subject to judicial review anywhere?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes it is.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: How?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It is again subject to judicial review if a subsequent exhibitor wants to show the same film and without the classification, he may again submit it to the Board for review and then file his notice of nonacceptance and have it tried by the Court.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: That's if there is another exhibitor, but suppose there isn't any.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: The same exhibitor may do it.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: You mean --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: If he fails to file his notice of nonacceptance --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: He may then resubmit --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- he can come in for a review.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: You mean he can resubmit the picture?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: He can resubmit. (Voice Overlap) He has given that right under the ordinance, if he so desires. We have tried countless things with every precaution possible in a field like this. Now --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Suppose he just go to him and shows the picture and doesn't submit to the Board?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: If he goes ahead and shows the picture without submitting it to the Board, then under our view of the case --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Under the law?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Under the law, under the ordinance, he has committed a misdemeanor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Committed a crime if he doesn't submit the picture for this Board to look at?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: If he shows it without submitting it, that's correct sir. That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do you say that's not censorship?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No sir, we do not think that's censorship.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, I understood the charge to be made if he did. I -- I couldn't quite understand it.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, no we don't think that's censorship, Your Honor (Voice Overlap), we actually -- actually, this was the same question that was in the Times Film case (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, whether you call it censorship or not, certainly in Freedman, we have sustained the constitutionality of procedures like is not violating the First Amendment, as long as the procedures we specified in Freedman are followed, haven't we?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And as long --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: It doesn't matter whether you call it -- so I think myself for this censorship where it's a kind that --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Allowable censorship maybe sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Alright, that's what Freedman held, wasn't it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes. So maybe I'm wrong in saying (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Let's get to the word --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- saying it's not censorship it --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Let's get to the word in the Constitution. It is an abridgement of the right of free speech, isn't it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: But illegal abridgement, yes sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But it is an abridgement, isn't it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It is a possible abridgement, yes if --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Possible, is it a possible or is it an abridgement?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Sir, it depends if the words of the Consti --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did I understand it, do you say it's -- it's not certain as an abridgement if the government, state or municipal demands that somebody before they say something or show a picture, turn it over to the law to be looked at by Board? Do you say that leaves the question of whether it's an abridgement of free speech?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I'll say, Your Honor, let's assume that that is an abridgement. I'll say that is an abridgement of this sort. But it depends upon whether or not the Constitution is actually --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Believes what it said?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, no. Not in that sense, sir. Whether or not it means unfettered, unlicensed right to speak at any times under any circumstances under any condition and say what one thinks. But I do not think that this is the interpretation that has been put upon that provision of the Constitution down through the years that there have been some abridgements that have been recognized as legal ones, as such. Obscenity itself if the Constitution says --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I thought that the argument there was that that really was a speech, as just kind of exploiting words.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I understand that but here again sir, is an interpretation and the clear and present danger doctrine is another interpretation that is what we're talking about. So we say that there are some circumstances under which free speech in some condition under which it may not be unfettered as such.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: How does the Board or Council -- how many are they?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: There are nine members sir and I believe at the present time, there are there alternate members.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do they elect them?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: They are appointed by the city council and they are required to have certain backgrounds in art and in literature and in philosophy and this sort of thing and some wide range of backgrounds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are they removable?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: They are removable, yes sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: By what?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: By the city council as such and this is the way it has actually worked.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What are their occupations?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What are their occupations?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: The occupations of those that are presently serving on it are a housewife, a preacher, a public relations man, a builder in construction, a real estate individual -- one that is versed in real estate matters and real estate business, a writer, a writer and interpreter, and the generally this is the background of these individuals.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I assume then none of them have any political affiliation?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No sir, they do not for this purpose.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What about the housewife?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: For this purpose.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What about the housewife?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I do not know what are political affiliations are, I've never heard their expressions, because actually the Dallas system is a little different maybe and that we do not have political parties insofar as city council is concerned, it's nonpolitical in that particular respect.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You just have the ins and the outs.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: The ins and outs, that's right sir. Maybe that's political in the sense, but that's what we actually have sir.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: All these people serve without pay, don't they? The members serve without pay?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Without pay, sir. Without pay and there's quite a burden on them because when the movie submitted, it must be done within the time limit and if --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: They see a lot of movies for nothing, don't they?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, they get rather tired of them Your Honor, but they do, and they take their own time and they actually see these at a time in which they are actually available to them and made so by the industry.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Just --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- a matter of interest, do they have their own projection room or something?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No sir, they do not have. The industry has -- has made the projection room available to them and told them at what times it will be available and they make themselves available at that time.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Yes, with the local theaters or something?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: The local theater people, yes sir. And in their viewing rooms, they have viewing rooms in Dallas in which they view movies that come in from all parts of United States for the exhibitors themselves to view to see whether or not they want to make the contracts in these sorts of things. So this, we realize is not in every city. Every city doesn't have these exhibitors --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Is this movie here for us to see?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes sir. The movie is available as I understand it that the industry has made it available for this Court.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: How many did you say -- how many movies did you say they had reviewed in a year?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Your Honor, they have had submitted to them 1900.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Beg your pardon.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: 1925 in a two-year-period.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: In a two-year-period?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: So they have -- they have actually sat through 1900 of those of pictures, is that correct?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Not actually, no sir. They have not.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, how they -- how do they pass on them if they haven't seen them?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Many times a movie is submitted as classified suitable for young persons. These people all do research and background. They get all of the information they can from the writers and from the reviewers throughout the country and submit these things, and then they consider this. And if there's no doubt about it and they can see no doubt about it or no indication of it, they do not actually view the movie as such.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: But they don't classify it as unsuitable without seeing it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No sir. If they're going to classify it as unsuitable, it must either be because the industry has said we submitted as unsuitable.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Does that happen?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Your Honor, it happened in 565 out of 604 cases.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: 565 out of 1925 over two-year-period I have?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes (Voice Overlap) well, yes that is correct but of the 604 that were designated not suitable --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: 39 by the Board and 564 by the exhibitors?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Supposing a movie sets by on the scene and were upon to the members of the Board viewing on Saturday night as free, to see the movie (Inaudible).

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Justice Harlan, you understand human nature very well, I see that because this has happened. And when it has happened they have then gone back and asked the Board to view the movie and have told that movie industry, they're doing it, and asked them to come in and actually present any evidence they desire to do so. May I point out that this is an open hearing at all times. The press is always present when they're discussing these movies, that the exhibitor and the producer or anyone else is requested and asked to come in, submit any evidence they want to, and present any ideas they might have about these movies. And we think that this is the way it should be.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Are they sometimes some of these cases are settled by the distributor or exhibitor agreeing to delete certain scenes and that sort?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: We have not attempted to ask the exhibitor to delete any scene.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Had they ever offered to do that?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: If so, I'm not aware of it sir.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, when the distributor submits a picture or an exhibitor does as the unsuitable for young persons as you say it that happened in 565 times, do they submit it as unsuitable in terms of the classification it has had in the distributor's private organization or do they submitted as unsuitable in terms of the Dallas ordinance?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Unsuitable in the terms of the Dallas ordinance.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: And they want to classify that way. I mean they don't object to it as (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: They do not object to it, as being classified that way. Now, I realize and let me be the first to say that counsel may say, well, this is under threat and I realize when the ordinance said there is some possibility of that and there would not. I don't think these many would've been submitted by the industry itself if there been no ordinance search.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: No, of course not.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Or if that --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: There wouldn't have been any permission at all --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: But --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: -- unless there's an ordinance.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's right. And so -- but at the same time I think it shows --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: At least in 565 times the industry said that we just don't want to litigate it.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, I think it's more than this.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: May I ask -- what?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I think it's more than this, more than just saying we don't want to litigate. I think that actually it's also saying that we recognize that these under this ordinance are not suitable.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: In terms of the --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Now, what do they offer to --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I beg your --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: If they think it's not suitable, why wouldn't they just not use them if that's here?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, there's still profit in one whether it's not suitable or whether it is suitable.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But, once they offer it to the Board and say it, “We want to show it but we recognize and come to you and tell you it's not suitable for the young persons.” I don't understand that procedure, why they wouldn't do it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I'm sorry. I don't understand Mr. Justice Black --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What I understand is when the exhibitors want to exhibit a picture, comes up and says, “Now, here I'm submitting to this Board, for examination, look at this picture, I myself do not think I want to offer it. It should not be shown.”

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, no.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: It's a bad picture to show.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, no. No --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But we're going to offer it to you. Why don't they do that?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No. Your Honor, this is not the way it works. They submit it as not suitable and say we request permission --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Not suitable for children?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Not suitable for young persons.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: And we are willing to exhibit it in Dallas with the label on it in our advertising not suitable for children under the ordinance.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's correct. And in the case --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: And not to sell tickets to children?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's correct. We have complied with the ordinance so far as this movie is concerned, and in that case, the Board itself does not actually see the movie. The Board itself does not actually do other than to accept the classification that's put on it by the movie industry itself.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What you're saying is, isn't in that idea meeting their consideration, they would be satisfied to have this picture exhibited by -- with the statement that we don't think it's suitable for children, but that wouldn't be good to be saying -- it is going to enter into a legal agreement, somebody could be tried if a child went, is it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Your Honor, I think this -- I think if a --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, the Board entered (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- the Board had acted on it which they do in those cases and had acted on it. And then they submitted this classification and then they did allow children to come and view it without their parent or guardian or husband or wife, yes, I think under the ordinance they could be prosecuted in the corporation courts, with the fine up to $200, this is the limits of the penalty involved in the case.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Do they've got larger ordinances where they say not suitable for children?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: You say, are there larger ordinances?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, do they get -- do the motion picture operators get bigger crowds when they say this is not suitable for children.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice Warren, just last Saturday -- last Friday, I received the phone call that told me that this was a fact. I do not know it to be so, that sometimes this brings in more crowds and more profit than without. I can't -- I can't actually substantiate it one way or the other. I would say this so, I'd say that the social opprobrium referred to by counsel does not attach to a label such as this. It is not indirect censorship as he refers to in that particular manner and I would cite for an example, if I might, something that's well known to all of us and that is the bars where liquor is served and the fact that young people are not admitted to those, certainly does not prevent many people patronizing those bars. We do not think that there is a social opprobrium attached to this thing.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Am I wrong in thinking that there's nothing in the Constitution that protects the sale of liquor such as there is to protect freedom of speech.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I think you're right Mr. Justice Black. I think you're right.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Bickley, I gather this under 18 ordinance, municipal ordinance, that's been repealed, has it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That has been repealed, yes sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: And replaced by this under 16?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That is correct. Now, I know there's been a lot to do and made about that fact. We hope that in the passage of the ordinance and in the actual application of it that we're doing everything we can to make it a sensible one and actually to balance if you will in whatever sense we have to and whatever sense we need to the right to protect the young person.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do you think --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: But at the same time give --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do you think that the way a parent can and if he wants his child to see a picture, he's 16, he can let him see it if you go with him. But if he doesn't go with him, it's a criminal offense?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: This is correct, Your Honor. This is correct. Now, --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: That's a criminal offense only for the exhibitor?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Only --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Not for the parent?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, only for the exhibitor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Neither the parent nor the child is --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What about the boy that goes?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: There is an offense to the individual who falsifies his age or who fraudulently -- fraudulently attempts to get in and does get in.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But of course he's 15 and he's asked to the cashier, I'm 15, the cashier says, go in and he goes in --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Then --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- the boy subject to any offense?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: The boy is not subject to any (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But the exhibitor is?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: The exhibitor subject to the --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, if the boy just walks up and buys a ticket and just sold to him no questions asked, no offense either?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, no offense in that way. We in fact actually --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: No offense if he goes to see it? Without his father?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No. There's no offense in that particular case unless he falsifies in order to see it.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, suppose he got in -- suppose he doesn't just tell it direct falsification, but that he goes up and buys ticket, he's represented and he's old enough to go in there.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, this is a representation of a sort, yes sir, I admit that but --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And he could be charged?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Not such that -- it could -- if he could be proven yes, it be possible but actually you cannot find a juvenile of this kind any how (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Have you had any prosecutions under this one?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No prosecutions.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Of anybody?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Not of anybody. The -- I would like to state to this Court that attempting to comply with what the law is whether we like it or not that all parties have done their utmost in trying to get the thing in such a way that actually we could present it as a law question and that we could finally come out with some rules and guidelines on this new type of concept and it is a little bit new. Under which we can operate, protect our young people if you will, but at the same time grant to all individuals all of the First Amendment freedoms that we can and that should be granted to them. Now, this is not an easy concept.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You haven't yet tried to censor any books that may have story like this what you say is bad in this particular picture.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No sir, we have not --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) who read this?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: We have not concerned ourselves with books, we think here again -- here again and we have a state statute on it so therefore we could not even if we so desired. The city could not, but the state could. But we have not concerned ourselves with it because we think this goes in to the home and here again the parent has the right to take care of his own child, the same as the TV movies that were referred a minute ago.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, why doesn't he have the same right to take care of his child by letting him go to a movie if he wants to without going with him?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: For this reason we think, Your Honor, and right or wrong, we think it is a valid concept that he cannot explain to his child the difference between right and wrong or the difference between morality and immorality or sexual promiscuity and smut unless he's there with him.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Oh, you expect to be that way and explain it to him as they go along?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Not as they go along, Your Honor. Not as they go along, but in order to -- to actually fulfill his duty as a parent, we think that this is one of the things that should be required and we do not want to impinge on that right of the parent if we can keep from doing so and we hope that it's not necessary --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How long is this ordinance when its 18-year-old one to 16-year-old one, I mean the total span, how long has it been enforced?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Approximately it was passed first in April of 1965, there's a first passage.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did it get to referendum?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, it did not go to referendum and it was not necessary.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: How much does it cost you, how much does it cost a child to be admitted to this intellectual and emotional feast in the theatre there? What's the admission charge?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Sir up until 12 years of age, I believe, it's 60 cents or some such thing and a dollar-and-a-half afterwards, it varies.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: We'll recess now.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice Warren and Associate Justices of this Court, I would like to clear up a misstatement that I made to this Court in my argument this morning. On page 126 of the record in the ordinance, makes it unlawful for any young person to enter or remain in the viewing room of any theater where a film classified not suitable for young person is being exhibited. I misinformed the Court, I apologize.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: So this means that a young person of 16 who happen to get in will be prosecuted?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: For what?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: He would be -- he would be prosecuted for actually violating the ordinance and that's all.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) but what -- what's the punishment?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, the only punishment that can be given to a person of that age is to turn them over to move to juvenile authorities actually. You cannot fine him. You cannot put him in jail. Really there's -- it's like many of our other juvenile halls, it's rather hard to enforce actually. It would only be one of a number of things that would have to be proven maybe in a District Court later on to show that it was a delinquent or that maybe he was not properly taken care of by his parents and this sort of thing. But this is about all you could do in a case like that.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Suppose it'd be in a nature of truancy, wouldn't it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Same and similar type of thing and that's about all --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- we can do, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That also provides the punishment for anyone who knowingly permits him to view it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Knowingly permits him, that is correct and knowingly is a hard proposition but it must be proven. It would have to be --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) so suppose his father permitted him knowingly.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Without his presence, then his father would be actually the one that would be prosecuted in a situation like that.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, if he will be prosecuted, what's the fine?

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Does it --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Up to $200-fine.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: No -- no imprisonment?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No imprisonment.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Then knowingly permit comes under Section 46 (a) (4) (a) which as I read it applies only to an exhibitor or his employee. It should be unlawful for any exhibitor or his employee knowingly--

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- to permit any young person to view that.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That does apply to the exhibitor or his employee. The part which applies to the father would apply to the father is over in Section (c) on page 127. It would be a case in which he gives to a young person a ticket to go to the movie or purchase it for him and take it or some such thing as this.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I think you had there have been no prosecution of any kind under this ordinance.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, there have not, Your Honor. The one thing I think we need to keep in mind and I reiterate to the Court is the fact that we view this as a classification ordinance and not as a censorship ordinance because there is no restraint on the content of speech as such. The only restraint is on the audience that may actually view this content.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: So, why wouldn't that be censorship if I -- if playing with words the Court took that, why wouldn't that be censorship?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: First Amendment gives the right to speak and the right to hear.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes, I understand Mr. Justice Black it does. It also gives a right of choice and in this case, no individual is prohibited from seeing it. The conditions are the only thing -- their conditions are the only thing.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And this condition is the right to see it.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Their condition is right to see it under certain circumstances. The same thing as we do when we talk about free speech in a parade. A parade cannot be held at all times under all conditions when any individual, any 10,000 individuals desire to hold them because there are other freedoms and there are other rights that must be given some --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That-- it's quite a different thing, that's doing something plus free speech.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Just marching up and down.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What's the difference?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Alright, may I refer to the movie theater if I might as this Court has referred to it and that's hollering fire in the movie theater in which situation, Your Honor, I think it's not unlimited free speech.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, he wouldn't have a right to go in there and disturb the people. Nobody would.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Under the speech --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's speaking when he wants to and as he wants to and under the conditions (Voice Overlap) as I see it Your Honor and it's this -- it's this reason, I say it, this what makes hard law makes it tough and these cases are not easy, if they were. This Court would not be having to seat on, as many as it has to, at this particular time.
