But this is a time in history when I think we need to look at this type of law to see whether or not the Constitution as we know it is a document alive, if it can support a nation that not only has a past but must have a future.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What do you mean by that?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I mean by that simply this, Your Honor, that to strictly construe and without any fetters whatsoever, this can be the sort upon which we impale ourselves rather than the sort upon which we fight for our country.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You mean the judges should adopt it to meet circumstances?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I think it must under certain circumstances and in light of present day circumstances, just exactly what this Court did in the Robel case.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What's the purpose of the written Constitution if the Court can change it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor if we had any instrument that was not subject to interpretation --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I'm not talking about interpretation, interpretation in explaining what you think it means and it means when it came out?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes sir, I understand.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: It's quite a different thing --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) to take the position, that the Court should adopt it to meet circumstances of the time even though it didn't do that as it was written.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I think this -- I think that if and this is a personal opinion on my part, I think that if this is an instrument that has no latitude --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: No what?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- no latitude.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You mean --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- if the Court must obey as written?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No Your Honor, I say if it's an instrument that has no latitude.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, that's what --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: By that I mean, it has --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- if it has no latitude if it's to be construed according to its meaning --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: You're right.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- but if it has no latitude, it's just there and --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And the --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- it applies, that's the way it should be amended.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And Your Honor if it's there and if it's there in unfettered terms like that then I have a right to disturb any meeting I want to at any time and under any circumstances.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do you? Why would you have a right to go into a church and disturb that meeting but the church own the church?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor under the freedom of speech, if it were an untrammeled right.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That's the freedom of speech where you have a right to speak.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Your Honor --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That's not -- that doesn't allow you to come in here for instance and make a speech or go into a church, you can't do that, that's not freedom of speech. That's freedom to go where you please even though somebody else owns a property in our system.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, then under freedom of speech I'd have a right maybe to get me a megaphone and stand outside and speak into that church --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, would you? Why wouldn't you?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- outside, (Voice Overlap) I say you do not have that right.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, why wouldn't you have that right?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I don't think I do.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And I don't think this --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I mean, if I --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: --this Court thinks I do.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- if I have to agree with you, then why wouldn't you have that right?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: But I'm saying this is because of the enlightened interpretation that's been put on the words in the Constitution.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, I think it's because you follow the Constitution as it's written.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, I think maybe I'm not communicating properly with the Justice, but I think this is so, yes. You're following it as written and as written it must have the mind of men applied to it. And I think that's what we know.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: The mind of men applied to it, to interpret it and enforce it as written but not to change it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, I don't think --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Why don't judges have anymore right to change it than anybody else.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Your Honor, I don't think this Court has changed it and I don't think this is --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, I agree with you --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- a change of it.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- not much.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. Bickley --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: -- I have great trouble with your crying fire in the crowd at theater, I think they all agree that you can't do it.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: But if somebody does cry fire, I have a right to listen to it, don't I?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Generally, (Voice Overlap), it's advisable to listen Mr. Chief -- Justice in these circumstances.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, this --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: You do have a right (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) somebody to look at something.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: You do have a right to listen to it, that's correct because --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: In this case we have the right to look at the movie.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Everybody has a right to look at it.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Not an unescorted child under 16.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Not an unescorted child under 16, that's correct.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: So --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- neither does an unescorted child under 16 have a right to make many other choices. Your Honor, may I cite one for example.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: I'm listening.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: As this Court has held in the case of the inoculations given to the children at school even though it's against his religion. The child has no right to make the choice.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, at this -- I don't know whether the parent could make this choice.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: The parent can't even make that choice, Your Honor. So, I'm saying that that are --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, let me see how you get that over into this case. This case is a picture that you aren't banning, you're just restricting --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: -- the right of people to see it.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: And it's done without any given reason at all.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Your Honor, it's correct. They -- the Board has not given a written reason as to why. They have done it under the confines of the ordinance.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: How do I know that? I take their word for it.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, yes in that particular situation, the same as -- as when a court quite often makes a decision without giving all of the reasons for it and none of our District Courts --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Now, let's get our answers correct. You said without giving all of the reasons?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: But in this case they didn't give any reason.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor, except they said that we -- the Court actually said that he thinks we were entitled to an injunction on the law.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: No, I'm talking about the Board itself.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: The Board itself gave no written reason. And the only reasons given were those that were given in the Court by two members of the Board, you're correct Your Honor.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: I have a great trouble --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And this is maybe -- this is maybe something that should be more closely drawn as you actually said in the Robel case when you're trying to decide between the validities of the two things, the legislative power on one hand and the individual rights on the other. Maybe you draw a narrow line and maybe this is one of the lines we missed, we tried not to but it's possible. And certainly we understand the intent of it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Bickley, I'm just wondering if it isn't -- but then the situation where neither the Board nor the Court makes any findings. Now, I suppose you have four of your board members think that one scene in the picture takes it and the other five of it, don't think that one does but they do think another scene affects it, but they just put them together and say, “Well, it's bad”. And then they go to the Court and the Court thinks well maybe the first one is alright, but the second one is wrong. Now, has the Court passed on anything that we should reach?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I don't understand the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, I mean --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- Mr. Chief Justice question at length --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I mean that, is there any -- is there any basis for us determining whether they acted rationally or lawfully in what they did --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Oh! Yes sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- when it comes to us? What -- what is it we act upon?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes sir, I think there is.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You mean because the Supreme Court of the state guessed from -- from what the trial court decided and the Board decided made it bad?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No, I don't think that --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: What does?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- Mr. Chief Justice. But I think the thing that this Court has to act though is the evidence that was presented, the ordinance itself in the face of the Constitution and the writings of this Court that they have every opportunity on earth to determine whether or not that the ordinance itself is valid and whether it's properly drawn. Now, as to its application to this particular movie, I would assume that this Court as it has in some other situations I understand, may would want to see the movie itself to see whether or not it was properly applied because as I understand it, you must make that final decision.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But without any findings in the courts below to support us?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I think there are findings Your Honor. In the Court of Civil Appeals opinion, the Court was very explicit in saying what they based their decision on. And this was a matter that went up to the Supreme Court of the State of Texas. Supreme Court of the State of Texas in (Inaudible) would simply meant that they didn't agree with everything but they thought they reached a right conclusion and it was a upon -- it's on this record that it comes to this Court then and the final arbiter as to whether or not this movie meets the standards.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, Mr. Bickley against what standard are we to test our view of this motion picture?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I think from a standard in the ordinance if the Court finds it as a constitutional standard which we think it is.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: In other words, we're not going to test it against any standard of obscenity?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I don't think it is a complete obscenity question Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, --whenever.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- we have viewed motion pictures before, it has been in the context of an argument that motion picture is not obscene within the definition of obscenity that the Court has fashioned.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: That is not the way we'd --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: No.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- look at this. Well, now how can we know, what are we to take, out 47 different standards or something in this ordinance, some about Mr. Nizer said there were great number? Are we to test to see whether it complies with anyone as we view what the standard means without your courts telling us what it means or without the Board telling us which of the several standards would apply when it --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It's possible --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- passed by this?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: It's possible Mr. Justice Brennan that the Courts were remised in this respect --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Oh, remised but --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And it -- and I think -- what I think this Court --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But have a little sympathy for our shore --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I do have.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- well, what are we -- well, what are we going to do? We don't know what under which this was classified, do we, because we haven't been told --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, the way you put it though, there's not even a federal -- we wouldn't even be dealing with the federal question, you said once you decide the statute's constitutional, you just apply the ordinance, that is just a state law question and why we would even be concerned with it at all. Should we really be concerned of whether the ordinance is valid on its face and if so --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: -- has it been constitutionally --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- derived --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: -- applied in this case. Now it doesn't make --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: -- any difference to you, I take it, whether the Court approaches this case is saying that you may -- that perhaps the First Amendment allows the state to restrict the access of children to non-obscene matter or whether obscenity is applied to children simply has a different meaning. Do you care -- would you care if the Court whether you call this obscenity or not you, you don't see how you would care if the Court says -- if the Court says, “Well this is an obscenity case, It's a question of --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: I would get --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: -- of the question of obscenity for children.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Mr. Justice White, I would hope the Court would do what it has indicated and maybe on one occasion and that is that that material that is harmful to children or that can create problems with children maybe ought to be included in the definition of obscenity. If this is what you're talking about then I'm -- I certainly can agree with the Court if we're talking about a definition similar to obscenity for adults and with all of the requirements that it has there, I think it will not --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: But this --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- meet the end that it must meet.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: In this particular case as you view, you've seen this picture, it's -- it's the invitation to sexual promiscuity that makes -- that raises the question about --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: -- this picture?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: And that is in the obscenity feel, I take it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That is correct, sir. That is correct.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, I suppose you would say -- I suppose at least partly respectable argument could be made that under the United States Constitution, a state or a municipality would be constitutionally free to say that no children under 16 could go to any movies at all say after 8 P.M. unless accompanied by their parents or could say that no children under 16 could go to movies unless accompanied by their parents that the Board had expressed an opinion about were unsuitable for children based on -- maybe the Board might say that all we want to children to see in our community are Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs. Quite without reference to obscenity is what I'm trying to say.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes, I understand.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Isn't that your real (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: What we're referring to --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- that our obscenity cases are not truly relevant to this case, isn't that what you're trying --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. That is correct, that they are not truly relevant to this problem and --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- to the thing that must be taken into consideration in this case.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, what are the limitations on the Board then in determining what is good for children and what isn't? If obscenity isn't the test, obscenity as it applies to children is not the test, what test is there for the Board?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Your Honor, we have attempted to establish a test in the ordinance whether or not it's constitutional is of course a question that is --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- now being presented.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Or you heard --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Could he answer that please?

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Oh! Excuse me.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And to determine what the test should be, we're trying to apply of the law we can muster to try to arrive at this and --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And what you have mustered, that's what I --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Well, the only thing we been able to muster at this point are the indications by this Court that this is a special problem and a legitimate interest of the state and that is the protection of its young people.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Confined within the Senate.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And then in the context -- yes, in the context of the obscenity cases for adults, then we've tried to adapt it over as best as we can and without going too far we hope in this respect. Now, to do that is just as hard as it is to define obscenity as such and to determine what is obscenity? We know this and for that reason maybe inaptly sometimes we have expressed it. I cannot answer with certain definiteness the Mr. Chief Justice's question because I'm not sure that it can be answered as a definite factor. I hope that we can get guidelines in which we can reach more definiteness.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, Mr. Bickley, I -- it's like asking you so many questions -- but when you say you drew on something this Court said, are you -- do you have reference to what -- in opinion in Jacobellis said on this subject? Is that what you think (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: We are -- we're hoping that we had properly interpreted.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: No, but when you said that there was something that this Court said --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes, what this Court --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- you're speaking of what was said in Jacobellis?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: In Jacobellis, yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well now, that of course was in the context of obscenity.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: That is correct. That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But I thought you just said and answered --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- Mr. Justice Stewart that you're trying to reach for something beyond --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- the context of obscenity.

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: And we're trying to move it from the obscenity for adults over to the protection of children without doing violence to the First Amendment as best we can.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: But getting beyond the obscenity area?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Your Honor, we've talked about obscenity here and if we're talking about obscenity as it can be defined so far as it concerns children and we think that this is generally what we've done in this ordinance then we have no quarrel with that. No quarrel with that.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Do you think what you've generally done really is focus on obscenity in the context of exhibition to children --

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: Of exhibition --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- is that it?

turns.name: N. ALEX BICKLEY
turns.text: -- to those below the age of 16 years. This was the same thing that Mr. Justice Hughes in the Federal District Court and the Circuit Court in New Orleans said the due obscenity rules if applied to this statute would be proper.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Nizer?

turns.name: LOUIS NIZER
turns.text: Thank you, sir. The standards which this ordinance sets forth have in many areas already been rejected by this Court. It might be worth the moment to look at the standards themselves. In determining that a picture is not suitable for children, the Board must first determine whether the picture describes or portrays brutality, criminal violence or depravity or nudity or sexual promiscuity or extramarital or abnormal sex relations. Second, if the picture describes any of these phenomena, the Board must determine whether the portrayal is such that it is likely “to insight or encourage” crime or delinquency or actual promiscuity in the part of the young persons. Third, in determining whether it is likely to insight or encourage crime, delinquency and so on, the Board must determine whether there is in its judgment “a substantial probability that it will create an impression on young persons that such conduct is profitable, desirable, acceptable, respectable, trustworthy and commonly accepted.” Now, identical of similar standards have been condemned by this Court and other courts as too vague to satisfy constitutional guarantees. For example, “sexual immorality” People against Bookcase; “prejudicial to the best interest of the people”, the Gelling case; “Creating a harmful impression on the minds of children”, Paramount against Chicago stricken out; “Sexual immorality is desirable, acceptable, or improper patterns of behavior”, Kingsley stricken out, and some of these cases of course have dealt with the protection of children. That doctrine that there may be variable obscenity has never been extended to mean that there can be variable vagueness and this statute is void and defective completely because of vagueness. Just two or three other brief points, it has been suggested here for the first time that my client United Artist may protest and has standing before this Court. That is not so at page 128 of the record without reading it subdivision 7, only in exhibitor may file a notice of nonacceptance, not a distributor and indeed the exhibitors as this Court has observed takes little to deter the exhibitor from going to this kind of a contest and that one occasion we've even had to threaten to sue the exhibitors so we've filed as nonacceptance so we could get an intervening application. Second, with respect to the --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Mr. Nizer, to what extent had distributors participated and submit in these voluntary submissions?

turns.name: LOUIS NIZER
turns.text: None at all. And we have protested against them in many instances and we have no standing before the Board.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Do you know how often the -- how often the -- let's say in the 565 submissions that unsuitable, how often those 565 correspondent with the distributor's classification of their own (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: LOUIS NIZER
turns.text: In very few, if any though I have not the statistics before me but I will go further Mr. Justice White. The exhibitor has yielded against his will in these matters. There is this matter of fighting the authorities of a community in which you live and depend upon the good will of it and this very existence of this ordinance is itself a suppression. There would not have been 39 overrulings in the exhibitors who's finally saying this is alright with me when he submits a specific request for a suitable classification and it's overruled, and he says let's go along because he doesn't want to spend the money for counsel to come up here. One other observation about the Freedman case which I think we owe to Your Honor, a candid statement about it. We don't think that the Freedman case has been complied within consequently, this statute is void in any event in this application to the reasons I have given that you can have a complete final determination without any judicial participation here. But I would -- I think we owe to this Court to be candid to say that the motion picture industry has never been able to adjust itself to the proposition that while other medias such as magazines, newspapers, television even comic books may not be restrained by prior consideration, prior submission that we cannot share that sunshine and that we stand in the shadow of a five to four decision which says that we alone, the motion picture industry comes into a special category. We would therefore respectfully suggest now that this has demonstrated the ineffectualness of the Freedman case in attempting to modify the effects of the Times case that if we went on the cause --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Excuse me, the Freedman was not five to four. Maybe it was seven --

turns.name: LOUIS NIZER
turns.text: No, the Times case was five to four. That's the one that --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: The Freedman is the one that's settled, isn't it? The proposition that there are distinctions which justify the measure of prior restraints with Freedman sanction --

turns.name: LOUIS NIZER
turns.text: I think it was --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: That -- that was not a five to four decision.

turns.name: LOUIS NIZER
turns.text: No, not that. The Times case which puts the motion picture industry in the special category was and I merely observed that I think while the Freedman case was an admirable effort to avoid the abuse by these judicial requirements that nevertheless it hasn't worked. Well, here we are on Viva Maria, if Your Honor if you see that picture you'll be shock that this picture could be caught in that kind of a net when it was intended for something else and next day it will be something else and then it will be as I've indicated a proliferation of these ordinances. I wish to observe that while it is true that in this case there's no salary to these people, that in most of these proposals, they not only receives salaries but there is a fee for submitting the picture for their examination and you get the entire bureaucracy imposed upon this. And I think that this will cause a great flood of this kind of case whereas if we -- if in dealing with this difficult question, if we subjected the restraint to at least to penal provisions or at most injunction and declaratory judgment with all the safeguards for a defendant with the presumption of innocence and beyond the reasonable doubt, then I think this perhaps might be held in control. And the penal statute would get after the man that should get after some case where somebody is really trying to overstep the bounds of sensibilities of the American people. This is not this case at all. It doesn't even come up on obscenity.
